topic: MCP tool/resource responsibility boundaries for Fabric CLI and mcp_server CRUD optimization

findings.finding: MCP ToolAnnotations include readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint and openWorldHint, but the spec frames them as hints rather than hard security controls.
findings.detail: This supports using annotations to improve client UX while still keeping high-impact local writes behind explicit CLI commands.

findings.finding: MCP resources are intended for server-exposed context, with optional listChanged and subscription/update notifications.
findings.detail: Fabric's bootstrap README resource and cache/resource notifications align with this model; future rule/ledger read surfaces can use resources/resource links rather than destructive tools.

findings.finding: MCP tools may return resource links and can be notified via tools/list_changed; tools are better for actions, resources for context.
findings.detail: This supports splitting rule discovery into tool-driven selection plus resource-backed context where useful.

best_practices.practice: Use MCP read-only tools for model-invoked context computation, and reserve destructive filesystem changes for explicit human-driven CLI workflows.
best_practices.rationale: Tool annotations are advisory; responsibility boundaries should not depend solely on client behavior.

best_practices.practice: Expose stable context as MCP resources where possible, and use notifications for changed resource/tool lists.
best_practices.rationale: Resources are the protocol-native context surface; notifications keep clients from using stale context.

pros: Agents can manage all Fabric state directly
cons: Harder to make user intent explicit
alternatives.verdict: Reject as default model; keep only compatibility or narrowly scoped append telemetry.

alternatives.option: Read-only MCP server plus CLI-only writes
cons: Agents need user/CLI handoff for changes
alternatives.verdict: Preferred target model with limited append-only telemetry.

alternatives.option: REST API as single source for all surfaces
pros: Dashboard and CLI could share HTTP semantics
cons: CLI would depend on a running server for local tasks
alternatives.verdict: Reject for CLI; share services instead of routing CLI through HTTP.

pitfalls.mitigation: Keep destructive writes out of default MCP flow and gate them via CLI or explicit compatibility tooling.

pitfalls.issue: Duplicating read/diagnostic behavior across CLI and HTTP
pitfalls.mitigation: Extract common service modules and let CLI/HTTP render differently.

codebase_gaps.gap: scan report generation duplicated between CLI and HTTP API
codebase_gaps.current_approach: CLI has config-aware i18n implementation; server API has separate hardcoded implementation.
codebase_gaps.recommended_approach: Move scan report construction into shared or server service and reuse from both surfaces.

codebase_gaps.gap: Deprecated write MCP tools remain registered alongside new read-only cognitive protocol
codebase_gaps.current_approach: Tool descriptions discourage use but tools are still available.
codebase_gaps.recommended_approach: Document compatibility status, add tests that new docs/workflows do not call them, and plan a removal/hidden phase.

sources.title: MCP Schema Reference - ToolAnnotations
sources.key_takeaway: Annotations are advisory hints for tool behavior.

sources.title: MCP Resources
sources.key_takeaway: Resources are the protocol-native context surface with optional change notifications.

sources.title: MCP Tools
sources.key_takeaway: Tools are model-invoked actions and may link resources.
